### Related literature {#sec-literature}

This paper sits at the intersection of real options, strategic investment, structural credit risk, and the economics of AI. I organize the review around the four building blocks and then state the marginal contribution.

**Real options and capacity choice.**
The foundation rests on @mcdonald1986value for optimal timing of irreversible investment and @pindyck1988irreversible for capacity choice.^[Other foundational contributions include @brennan1985evaluating, @bernanke1983irreversibility, @majd1987time, @barilan1996investment, and @abeleberly1996optimal on costly reversibility.] @guo2005investment model demand with regime-dependent drift and volatility, showing large investment jumps upon favorable regime arrival; @decamps2006irreversible study irreversible investment with alternative projects under regime switching, using a two-regime structure with an absorbing state that is close to the present paper's $L \to H$ transition. The key difference is that their framework does not include strategic interaction, training-inference allocation, or default risk.^[@bloom2009impact develops time-varying volatility shocks; @bloom2007uncertainty provide firm-level validation; @jovanovic2005general document S-curve diffusion for general-purpose technologies.]

**Strategic investment.**
@grenadier2002option shows that competition drastically erodes the option value of waiting.^[See also @grenadier1996strategic on development cascades, @back2009open on closed-loop strategies, @weeds2002strategic on R\&D delay, and @fudenberg1985preemption for game-theoretic foundations.] @huisman2015strategic is the closest strategic investment paper in which they study a duopoly with joint timing and capacity choice. I extend their framework with endogenous default risk, training-inference allocation, diminishing returns, and regime switching. @lambrecht2003real study real options in duopoly with incomplete information, where firms have private signals about the investment payoff; their setting is related to the present paper's heterogeneous beliefs about $\lambda$, but they do not model the training-inference allocation or the endogenous default mechanism that links beliefs to survival. @bouis2009multistage provide an $N$-firm sequential entry framework that could extend the duopoly analysis.

**R&D races and arms race dynamics.**
The investment dynamics in this paper also connect to the literature on patent races and R&D competition.
@loury1979market and @lee1980market model firms choosing R&D intensity to accelerate a stochastic Poisson breakthrough, showing that competition leads to socially excessive duplication of effort.
@reinganum1981dynamic and @reinganum1982strategic extend the framework to asymmetric firms, demonstrating that the frontrunner invests more aggressively---analogous to the leader's higher training fraction in Proposition 3(ii).
@harris1985patent and @harris1987racing model R&D as a race with position-dependent investment incentives, producing a "discouragement effect" for lagging firms that echoes the follower's reduced training allocation in the duopoly equilibrium.
@grossman1986optimal study simultaneous timing and scale choices in R&D competition under uncertainty, combining elements of the capacity and timing decisions modeled here.
The key departure from this literature is the dual role of capacity: in standard R&D races, investment during the race is pure cost with no concurrent revenue, whereas here, installed capacity generates inference revenue pre-switch and training quality determines competitive position post-switch, creating the allocation trade-off that is absent from existing race models.

**Structural credit risk and investment-financing interactions.**
The default mechanism builds on @merton1974pricing and @leland1994corporate.^[@blackcox1976valuing and @goldstein2001ebit extend to first-passage and dynamic settings.] The most closely related papers combining investment and default are @hackbarth2014capital (real options with endogenous default and product market competition) and @kumar2018optimal (joint leverage and irreversible capacity choice, showing they are substitutes).^[@sundaresan2015dynamic model growth options with default risk; @bolton2011unified and @bolton2019investment model financial constraints; @morellec2004can studies endogenous investment and capital structure; @strebulaev2007tests develops dynamic capital structure with infrequent adjustment; @hennessy2004tobin, @mauer1994interactions, @childs2005interactions, and @miao2005optimal further develop financing-investment interactions.] The present model integrates endogenous default into the strategic capacity-choice game, building on @hackbarth2012corporate for the investment-financing framework.

**AI economics and scaling laws.**
AI training exhibits power-law scaling [@kaplan2020scaling; @hoffmann2022training], motivating the concave production function; @jones1995rdbased and @bloom2020ideas study diminishing returns in idea production. On the training-inference allocation, @akcigit2018growth study exploitation versus exploration R\&D---the closest analog, but in a multi-product firm rather than a compute allocation setting.^[@berk1999optimal and @carlson2004corporate develop the growth option decomposition used in @sec-valuation. @katz1986technology and @farrell1986installed study technology adoption with network externalities and installed-base inertia. @babina2024artificial and @eisfeldt2024generative provide empirical context on AI investment and valuations; these papers could in principle discipline certain calibration inputs (e.g., AI firm betas, investment intensities), though the rapidly evolving nature of the sector limits the stability of such estimates.] On the macroeconomics of AI, @acemoglu2024simple, @korinek2024scenarios, and @jones2024agi analyze growth implications of transformative AI in general equilibrium. The present paper is partial equilibrium---it takes the demand process and discount rate as given---and therefore complements these general equilibrium analyses by providing a micro-founded model of the firm-level investment and allocation decisions that aggregate into the industry dynamics these papers study.^[@dellaseta2012optimal combine real options with learning curves; @novymarx2007operating shows that operating leverage amplifies systematic risk, relevant to the operating cost parameter $\delta$.]

**Marginal contribution.**
The mechanism that existing models cannot generate is the *training-inference allocation under regime-specific competition with endogenous default*: the firm's choice of how to split capacity between current revenue generation and future capability building, where (a) the relevant competitive variable switches across regimes (inference capacity today, training quality tomorrow), (b) the allocation determines competitive position differently across regimes, and (c) the allocation affects firm survival through the continuation-value channel in the default boundary. This mechanism produces two novel results: a *faith-based survival* condition (@eq-phi-underbar) in which training investment lowers the default boundary by raising the expected post-switch continuation value, and an asymmetric *Dario's dilemma* in which the training channel amplifies the cost of overinvestment relative to underinvestment. Neither result can be obtained from existing real options models (which lack the training-inference margin), existing strategic investment models (which lack endogenous default), or existing structural credit models (which lack the regime-switching growth option with capacity allocation).
